title: DELAYED ONSET NODULES. How to diagnose & treat lesions after dermal filler [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: DELAYED ONSET NODULES. How to diagnose & treat lesions after dermal filler [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: I once saw a patient who had a bony asymmetry that he thought was related to filler we got back some old pictures thankfully everyone's got pictures on their phone from years ago now and we found that exact lump on his face from years ago he just had an asymmetry he was anxious h…

segments.text: i once saw a patient who had a bony asymmetry that he thought was related to filler we got back some old pictures thankfully everyone's got pictures on their phone from years ago now and we found that exact lump on his face from years ago he just had an asymmetry he was anxious he spotted it knew we were going down the route of this being a complication and actually was just the way his face was he hadn't spotted it [Music] welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pierce hi i'm ryder pearce and today we're talking about delayed onset nodules something we all need to know a lot about generally uh but we're also heading into a phase possibly with respect to the vaccine and maybe with the pandemic that we may have more reactions to think about and certainly more to deal with so let's dive in and find out more so what do practitioners need to know about these delayed onset nodules well the first thing is don't assume immediately that it's a filler reaction that's probably the first thing that i see new practitioners do too

often which is any problem that they're presented with they immediately assume oh it's my treatment that's caused this problem there are many medical causes of lumps and bumps and you need to have your clinical hats on generally before you immediately start assuming that you've caused a complication with your treatment because many much of the time isn't that problem at all so how do you deal with someone who attends with a possible filler reaction so i mean most of the time they tend with a lump or a bump or something visual that they can't see now it's those that are trickiest actually because if you're if it really is a place that you know you're injected and there's lots of swelling that's it's quite a lot easier to make that diagnosis of their inflammation going on it's the milder ones where you need to make your first step discerning whether this is an inflammatory process or a non-inflammatory problem and that's the first thing that then puts you in a different route after from that point onwards so um inflammatory we all know the signs of inflammation but you've got to go looking for them to establish it so

especially a small lump one of the tests you might do is just to feel it like does it actually feel more tender than at than other areas because um one of the things you asked me just before we came on air was how do you tell the difference between inflammatory and non-inflammatory because it could just be a filler lump well one of the ways is to palpate it and if it feels tender that's a clue that there's some inflammation going on there because mostly filler on his own is not tender so that would be one of the first things figure out whether it's inflammatory or not and obviously some of that's down to the history so you could ask them a series of questions that look for inflammatory causes have you had a cough or a cold you know have you had any dental work anything that could cause inflammation um you may then start to lean in that inflammatory way but also what's the history of the lump did it come up um immediately after the procedure and has just got worse since then because that's very different to a lump that came on maybe three months after the procedure has come on gradually and is a little bit tender and that would also be different to a lump that has no tenderness and uh has no real signal that there's any infection but is also

there or present so they're different and but importantly different because each of those has a different possible cause and a different treatment how do we break it down and decide which route to go down um i'd start with questions first so is there any tenderness is there pain how did it come on what's the sequence of events you know i just woke up with it there's no tenderness um and that then you're leaning more towards non-inflammatory if you if it became a little bit sore and painful and then was swollen and you felt it and it feels a bit tender i'm immediately thinking that's more inflammatory right and that's gonna then take you off down the other path and when does a nodule a lump become a delayed onset nodule versus just a normal lump after the procedure yeah that's a good question what i always look for to differentiate delayed onset nodule and any other type of nodule is that there should have been a period where there was no inflammation and a recovery and i actually don't mind if that's three or four days although

that's on the shorter end or whether it's three six months but you need a period where things were seem normal and that's a good indication of um that it's not post procedural because every procedure if there's bruising you get you can get lump a bruise itself can result in duration and you get texture changes in the skin that feel like a lump but it's not reactive so um you're looking for that period of quietness where everything was fine and that's the key that differentiates it from uh procedural injuries you mentioned before that it might not be anything to do with our procedure what else could it be so it's as i said earlier on it's very important to keep your mind open when patients first present um it's one of the most common ways that clinical mistakes are made is that something happens in the history taking process that makes the clinician shut off their mind to many other differentials and they get tunnel vision i've seen this happen in a e when i used to work in amy where the sometimes the triage note

triage note on the front of the page would distort the entire consultation and you'd realize that triage note was either basically done in three seconds and it was would say something like chest pain and really once you got talking to them it's more like a gallbladder and you know you can waste ages because it's a rough description put in by the secretary that isn't that basically isn't um that sways everyone's thoughts way too quickly and patients do this themselves or sometimes ring up and say um you know i've got a headache and it turns out that it's more about eye pain and that's a really different history taking process than headaches so um you've got to think broadly at the beginning and so when it comes to lumps and bumps just think there are many things that can cause lumps and bumps and inflammatory processes that happen in people's heads that are nothing to do with dermal filler and the sooner you rule those ones out and then you can narrow in on your particular causes but um you know that one of the more typical ones you see quite often with new injectors is four die spots so those are just they're there already

but when you inject filler they become more readily visible so you get this increase in contrast or sometimes it's about rotation of the lip so you can see them more easily and new clinicians plus new patients suddenly think oh there's some problem with my filler and actually it's just something that was there all along but there are many examples of that so lymph nodes are another example lymph nodes come up in the face for any infection that may maybe anywhere in your head and they can feel like filler lumps if you're not careful about it and you get the diagnosis wrong you don't end up trying to highlight the lymph node for example um i once saw a patient who had a bony asymmetry that he thought was related to filler we got back some old pictures because i was convinced this was nothing to do with filler and i got his phone out thanks thankfully everyone's got pictures on their phone from years ago now and we found that exact lump on his face from years ago he just had an asymmetry he was anxious he spotted it knew we were going down the route of this being a complication and actually was just the way his face was he hadn't spotted it so it's fairly common um lips have loads

of these things so mucous seals are a good example you can get cysts of course if you're very lucky you might be dealing with a tumor so some cancers you know oral cancers might present like a lump that could be blamed on being a filler um and we just want to be just be aware of the other differentials before you zoom in on it too much and if it doesn't make sense quite often it's because there's something missing this is something that's always been useful to know in clinical medicine is when you're applying all your normal pattern recognition um systems and it just doesn't seem to fit like go with that it doesn't fit there's something wrong explore it more before you just ignore it and keep going down your initial diagnosis which may seem easier at first but it's definitely not easier if you get the diagnosis wrong assuming it is a delayed onset nodule because of the filler that we've injected what's causing it so delayed onset nodules are if only they were simple but there are multiple different subtle differences and theories about what's causing them so

any period of inflammation that happens in a filler um with a delay to it um is a delayed onset nodule and there are a couple of different theories but what could cause it so one of them is that your body is actually reacting to the filler so your immune system has learned that this is a foreign body and it needs to be destroyed and it basically you get a type 4 hypersensitivity response so cell mediated that's what it means type 4 you're sending in the white blood cells and they're trying to break it down they're causing an inflammatory response there's collagen deposition you effectively get scar tissue laid down and um and it can be fairly permanent those nodules that come from that if it's left for too long but there's another theory that maybe this is all caused by biofilm reactions so a biofilm reaction is a grumbling bacterial infection where your body can't quite get on top of it um there's this often in the textbooks it's to and fro between bit of inflammation and then it seems to go away and then it comes back again this waxing and waning can be part of it or it can just be an

infection that you cannot treat so it just seems to be resilient and the theory is well the fact is with these biofilms is that the bacteria coat themselves in a mucus and these biofilms are really interesting not if it's your face but they're interesting because they can have multiple organisms in them and they form almost like a mini um they're all kind of working together to defend against the immune system so you might have some that produce a type of mucus and some that produce an enzyme that defeats some element of the bacteria and together they also they all live on a foreign body in your system for as long as possible so um and that can look a lot like that some of the delayed onset nodules are probably those um trying to differentiate between the two is really hard in fact clinically it's next to impossible um it would be great if we could sample all of them but most clinicians are not in a place where we can take a sample send it to lab and even cultures are not that reliable and then there are you know you can sometimes um use and kind of look for the dna that's present in bacteria and try and scan that but obviously that's very

sensitive so you often pick up bacteria that's gotten in from somewhere else it's not always a concrete diagnosis either way so we're in this zone of uncertainty where we hopefully we know it's inflammatory we know from the history of our treatment that it's likely related to the filler and then we're in this position of is it a biofilm an infective related or just the body responding to it or the third thing which i i've been thinking about quite a lot and this is my hypothesis is that you can get inflammation that is more of a side effect of general inflammation rather than direct reaction to the filler and this is what i'm what i see differently clinically um one set of patients develops hard lumps that left untreated will persist and persist for months and there's another set of patients that develop inflammation that if you do nothing basically dies down with time um and then there are the ones that respond to antibiotics on their own which we may not know for sure but those would be um more related to

infection so we're trying to differentiate between these two and in practice i don't suggest that you run experiments i would treat it as both if you've got a hard lump so the hardness i think is important in terms of diagnosis if you have a general kind of soft swelling i think that might be more related to these flu type reactions so you get a flu infection you've got inflammation everywhere your back sore your legs are sore your you know there's that general inflammation and um it could be that short chain hyaluronic acids are a bit more likely to trigger some inflammation in that area and because that there is some evidence that we actually have receptors inflammatory receptors to short chain hyaluronic gases which also they occur during wound injuries so if you actually injure yourself there are more short chain hyaluronic acids in there and they're one of the mediators for inflammation so it could be that a flu infection or a viral infection or a vaccine predispose you to local inflam inflammation but it's not quite the same thing as your immune system saying i'm going after the filler

and that could be important because that means that you don't necessarily have to treat that as aggressively the ones where the texture is hard i think that's a good signal that you've got white blood cells rushed into the area so you're actually getting this build up of the immune cells so that it feels it doesn't feel like filler anymore and any clinician who's seen these will know immediately you know this isn't the filler that i put in it's rock hard and sometimes very nodular that for me is more likely to be one of the other two categories which is the not the um infection um possibly the biofilm reactions or the reactions the actual filler now what to do in that situation i think it's safe to treat them as both so you'd combine steroids and antibiotics and then and then give that a reasonable amount of time to respond to that before you decide to use highlights and that's another discussion we can have which is when to highlight why are these delayed onset nodules more common in lips well um that's certainly my experience that they're more common in lips there

are a number of possible reasons it could be i mean it could be that lips are very sensitive and any little lump gets diagnosed quickly and you know the patient complains about it if you've had if you're having a little bit of inflammation in your cheek and it's on the periosteum you may not notice it or you may not complain about it so it could be that we're more sensitive in that area but of course that area of your face is also much more there's much more immune activity going on there the whole of your gi tract in fact the whole one of the biggest parts of your immune system involves defending that mucous membrane in your gi tract so there are many more white blood cells active in that area and it could be that we just become more inflamed more easily there so we pick it up for that reason um it could also be that there are more these biofilm reactions because the mouth is full as we all know it's full of bacteria no matter who you are no matter how often you brush your teeth plenty of bacteria there that if they got into the wrong place might cause and it's certainly an inflammatory response and we know that from dental work it's one of the possible causes of these inflammatory reactions is dental work because you get a

temporary bacteremia so just an influx of bacteria that are quickly dealt with but they stimulate the immune system to respond and that causes problems should you deal with the lumps and lips any differently or is it all the same principles well i mean a non-inflammatory lesion is very different to deal with inflammatory lesion they're fairly straightforward you know massage um thyme highlights if you need to um whereas the inflammatory lesions if it's on the soft type i i'm and you can't find a focus for infection so this is a patient who had a cold last week today they're fine they have no temperature they have suddenly swelling in their lip or their tear trough or something like that they have no other signs of infection you could just treat the inflammation if it's bothering them but also it's not i don't think it's that unsafe to leave it and keep a very close eye but just to monitor them now this might be especially true for example the vaccine because as we talked about you probably don't want to be suppressing the response to

the vaccine or you may not get the immunity that we're after so if someone has a little bit of swelling after that you might just want to try your best to do nothing i do think the psychosocial impact's important so i don't just look at my patients as chunks of meat if someone's very upset that might make me lean in a bit harder and be a bit more aggressive with my treatment you know something big coming up a wedding or whatever there are many um psychosocial reasons why you might decide it's warranted to treat a little bit more aggressively to get the inflammation under control um but then we're in the realm of um with the harder nodules of combining antibiotics and steroids and beyond that into high into using hydron days but just on the psychosocial element though i think a lot of that can be helped with a consult a good consultation because i know myself when i've had the second time i had my lips done both times i had a lump that just went after about four weeks so quite a while i just let it do its thing i didn't get annoyed or wound up i just like well i've got my

lips done yeah so i mean you are in a unique position because you do get um uh extra consultation you've got someone 24 hours a day who will who can talk to you about it if you needed to yeah but i never asked i think people need to just be told that it's kind of not i don't know if i'm just thinking about harsh but i feel like people need to just let it sit yeah there's nothing wrong with saying we need to give it some time and if that takes away the anxiety and you're not worried about it and moreover you're not hampered in your day-to-day life um by what you look like then yes it's absolutely fine to let it run and i would recommend that because as a general principle in anything medical minimal interventions are a good idea i mean one of the things you learn with experience is that sometimes when you're trying really hard it's when you make things worse so we want to be edging forward and making careful decisions to intervene as little as possible while keeping the patients safe and making them happy but on the other side you can be too medically centered and i've seen clinicians leave cases where

you know often i get messaged by people who are trying to get second opinions and um you know they'll send me a picture and they look shocking you know they've got huge swelling around their jawline and the clitoris saying i'll see you in two weeks right a bit casual yeah now there is i'm being humble here and that clinician will have their reasons and i'm not saying i'm right and they're wrong necessarily but my way of approaching these things is it is a factor that the patient is upset like it's not simply that i know what i'm doing with what's going on with the cells that are reacting and i'm giving a treatment so it is still something that might make me take a different course if that patient is not functioning in their day-to-day life they're not going out they're avoiding people and it's two weeks before you see them again i would probably be a bit more aggressive in that case um and i do generally for that reason use hieronders a bit sooner than some of the guidelines say in those type of patients i don't think you can leave someone who's frantic um and can't function and isn't going to

work for a day longer than you need to and i know a lot of people don't use like using hyaluronidase for various reasons i actually think it's actually very safe as medication goes very few things other than allergy that i'm that have any good foundation as a side effect so we just need to um to choose when a good time is for particular patients taking into account the whole thing the biology the psychology and the social function so we've taken them through the steroids antibiotic we might have just had a bit of watching and waiting when do we highlight so um taking into account the psychosocial impact you may decide to higher one day sooner rather than later or you may decide this patient is very laid back and i can let the antibiotics and the steroids run and run their course but at a certain point you're gonna you the reason to use halondis is that you're not progressing good rule of thumb any for any clinician um is if things are looking like they're improving just keep doing what you're doing and don't start changing things but if

they're stuck and they're stuck for too long that's an indication to think about doing things differently so if i was going to use hyaluronidase there are a couple of things to think about firstly we don't 100 know for sure what's causing this it could be a type 4 reaction a sterile reaction or it could be an infection or a biofilm reaction now if you think about what hylorendez does um in nature so it's in bee stings it's in i think it's in some snake venoms it's basically a an enzyme that enables toxins to permeate through the tissue that's why bee stings include it only one of 52 things in the compound so you don't need to know all of them um but it's sometimes used in nature to enable these toxins to permeate and the same will be true if you have a bacterial infection so if you have a bacteria that's growing in a lump of hyaluronic acid and surrounded by white blood cells and then you inject this hyaluronidase it's going to liberate all of those bacteria and in theory you might be a bit safer if your patient is on antibiotics when you do that so with a delayed onset nodule if you

think it's inflammatory i really recommend that they're on antibiotics when you do that reversal process don't when you do that reversal process don't forget to like and subscribe and of course if you'd like to download an article written especially about delayed onset nodules that is available in a link in the description below thanks for watching bye
